options: A. Warm standby: scaled-down replica stack in the second Region, RDS cross-Region read replica promoted on failover, Route 53 ARC for traffic shift.
B. Backup and restore: snapshots copied nightly, manual rebuild in DR.
C. Multi-site active/active: full ALB+EC2+RDS Aurora Global Database in both Regions taking writes, with sticky routing.
D. Pilot light: only the database is in DR, application stack rebuilt from CFN on disaster.
explanation: Warm standby fits ~60s RTO / ~5min RPO and is cheaper than full active/active. Backup-and-restore (B) is hours of RTO. Active/active (C) meets RTO but is more expensive than required. Pilot light (D) typically can't hit 60s RTO due to scale-up time.
optionExplanations: Correct — warm standby keeps a scaled-down replica stack and a cross-Region read replica that can be promoted within minutes, with Route 53 ARC providing fast deterministic traffic shift; this meets ~60s RTO / ~5min RPO at lower cost than active/active.
Backup-and-restore produces an RTO measured in hours due to snapshot restore time and stack rebuild, missing the 60s target by orders of magnitude.
Active/active meets the RTO but costs noticeably more than warm standby, conflicting with the 'lowest extra cost' constraint.
Pilot light keeps only minimal infrastructure warm; rebuilding the application tier on disaster typically takes longer than 60 seconds.

question: An existing Lambda + DynamoDB system has cold-start latency causing intermittent 3-second p99 spikes. The function is JVM-based on a supported runtime. The team can't change languages. Which fix is most effective with least change?
options: A. Enable Provisioned Concurrency on the function and turn on SnapStart, which on supported JVM, Python, and .NET runtimes restores a pre-initialized snapshot to dramatically reduce cold-start time.
B. Add CloudFront in front to cache responses; the cold start will not be visible.
C. Move to Lambda@Edge so the function runs at all edges; cold starts disappear.
D. Switch to a Kinesis-driven trigger to avoid cold starts.
explanation: Provisioned Concurrency keeps init'd workers ready; SnapStart restores a pre-initialized memory snapshot and is available for Java (managed runtimes), Python, and .NET (Lambda SnapStart was extended beyond Java in 2024). Together they minimize JVM init time. CloudFront caches don't apply to dynamic per-request data (B). Lambda@Edge has its own cold-start dynamics and stricter limits (C). Trigger type doesn't address JVM init time (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Provisioned Concurrency keeps initialized execution environments warm and SnapStart (now supported for Java, Python, and .NET on managed runtimes) restores a pre-initialized snapshot, dramatically cutting JVM init time.
CloudFront caching does not apply to dynamic per-request invocations and does not solve cold starts for the underlying compute.
Lambda@Edge has its own cold-start behavior and tighter execution limits; it is not a general remediation for backend cold starts.
Changing trigger type does not reduce JVM init time, which is what drives the cold-start latency.

question: An existing on-prem application uses an NFS share for stateful data. The team has lifted-and-shifted compute to EC2 in one VPC across two AZs but kept NFS on a single EC2 instance. Outages have followed each maintenance window. What's the best fix?
options: A. Migrate the NFS share to Amazon EFS with Standard storage class and Multi-AZ; mount from each EC2 instance via NFSv4.1; lifecycle to EFS-IA for cold files.
B. Replace NFS with S3 mounted via s3fs-fuse on every EC2 instance.
C. Move to FSx for Lustre, which is POSIX-compliant and cross-AZ.
D. Add a second NFS EC2 instance with rsync nightly.
explanation: EFS Standard is multi-AZ NFSv4 by default, so this is the lowest-risk swap. s3fs has poor POSIX semantics (B). FSx for Lustre is for HPC scratch and is single-AZ in most options (C). rsync nightly leaves a wide RPO (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — EFS Standard is multi-AZ NFSv4 by default; existing NFS clients mount with no app rewrite, and EFS-IA lifecycle reduces cost for cold files.
s3fs-fuse offers poor POSIX semantics (no consistent locking/rename) and is unsuitable for shared state that an NFS-aware app expects.
FSx for Lustre is for HPC scratch and most deployment types are single-AZ; failover and persistence semantics differ from EFS.
Nightly rsync to a hot-spare NFS instance leaves a 24-hour RPO and does not provide AZ-level resilience for live data.

question: A team's existing data lake uses S3 plus Glue plus Athena. Storage costs are dominated by cold partitions older than 12 months that are queried only quarterly. Which optimization keeps query capability and cuts cost most?
options: A. Lifecycle older partitions to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval; Athena and Spectrum can still query.
B. Lifecycle older partitions to S3 Glacier Deep Archive; query via Athena directly.
C. Move older data to Redshift Serverless and delete from S3.
D. Compact older partitions into a single huge file with Snappy.
explanation: Glacier Instant Retrieval supports millisecond access for infrequent reads and is queryable directly by Athena/Spectrum. Deep Archive cannot be queried by Athena directly (B). Migrating to Redshift adds ingest cost and locks the data (C). Compaction alone doesn't shift storage class (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Glacier Instant Retrieval keeps millisecond read latency, is queryable in place by Athena/Spectrum, and slashes per-GB cost for partitions queried only quarterly.
Glacier Deep Archive requires a restore that takes hours; Athena cannot query it in place.
Migrating to Redshift adds ingestion cost and removes the data from the lake, locking it behind Redshift compute.
Compaction with Snappy improves query performance but does not change storage class or per-GB pricing.

question: An ALB-fronted EC2 fleet shows 95th-percentile latency spikes during traffic bursts. CPU is at 30% on average; the cause is queue depth at the load balancer due to slow targets. The team also notices many idle connections. What is the most effective improvement?
options: A. Tune target group health checks, enable connection draining, set ALB idle timeout appropriately, and switch the target group's algorithm to least outstanding requests; ensure HTTP keep-alive is enabled and the application's keepalive timeout is greater than the ALB's.
B. Replace ALB with NLB; NLBs do not queue and will drop slow targets.
C. Move to API Gateway with usage plans capped at 100 RPS.
D. Add CloudFront in front to absorb bursts.
explanation: Least outstanding requests routes new connections to less-loaded targets, evening tail latency; correct keepalive timeouts avoid 502s on idle connections. NLBs at L4 don't queue but they also don't help with slow targets (B). API Gateway throttle reduces success rate, not latency (C). CloudFront caches don't help dynamic latency (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Least Outstanding Requests sends new connections to less-loaded targets, evening out tail latency; matching keepalive timeouts (target > ALB) avoids 502s on connection close races.
NLB operates at L4 and does not queue at the load balancer, but it also does not solve the root cause — slow targets — and removes useful L7 features.
API Gateway with 100 RPS caps would protect upstream but at the cost of dropped requests, not reduced latency.
CloudFront caches only help cacheable responses; it does nothing for dynamic per-request latency at the targets.

question: An organization runs Aurora MySQL with a heavy read workload. Read replicas are saturated, and the application's connection count keeps maxing out the writer. Which improvement is most effective?
options: A. Front the cluster with Amazon RDS Proxy to multiplex application connections, scale out Aurora reader auto scaling on CPU, and route reads via the reader endpoint with the application driver.
B. Migrate to Aurora Serverless v1 to remove connection limits.
C. Increase the writer instance class; readers will inherit the new size.
D. Replace Aurora with self-managed MySQL on i4i instances.
explanation: RDS Proxy multiplexes connections, drastically lowering open connections to the DB; Aurora reader auto scaling adds replicas under load. Serverless v1 has its own connection ceilings and is being deprecated (B). Bigger writer alone doesn't fix read saturation (C). Self-managed loses Aurora benefits (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — RDS Proxy pools and multiplexes application connections so the writer sees far fewer open connections, and Aurora reader auto scaling adds replicas under read load.
Aurora Serverless v1 has its own connection limits and is being retired; it does not directly address read saturation.
Aurora readers do not auto-inherit the writer's instance class, and even if they did, a bigger writer does not solve read-replica CPU saturation.
Self-managed MySQL on i4i loses Aurora's separated storage layer, auto scaling, and managed operational features.

question: A retailer's architect has been told that their existing monolithic web app on EC2 is over-provisioned. Compute Optimizer suggests resizing 60% of the fleet. They want to act on the recommendation safely with no outage and capture future drift. Which is the best ongoing approach?
options: A. Apply right-sizing via blue/green Auto Scaling group rotations using AWS Systems Manager runbooks; subscribe Compute Optimizer findings to Security Hub or a Slack channel weekly; tag instances with intended size and use AWS Config to detect drift.
B. Modify instance types in place during business hours one at a time.
C. Disable Compute Optimizer to avoid noise.
D. Convert all EC2 to Lambda to avoid right-sizing entirely.
explanation: Blue/green rotation via SSM is the safest mechanism; ongoing review of Optimizer findings plus Config drift gives continuous improvement. In-place modifications cause restarts and are risky during business hours (B). Disabling Optimizer abandons the optimization (C). Lambda rewrite is out of scope (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — blue/green ASG rotations via SSM Automation avoid outages, weekly Compute Optimizer reviews keep the loop going, and Config drift detection catches future deviations from intended sizes.
In-place instance type changes require a stop/start and risk in-flight requests during business hours.
Disabling Compute Optimizer abandons the entire ongoing right-sizing program rather than addressing noise.
A wholesale rewrite to Lambda is out of scope for a right-sizing exercise on an existing monolith.

question: A team is reducing costs on a steady-state EC2 fleet of 800 instances spread across many sizes and families, in three Regions. They want maximum flexibility and sustained discount, with little ongoing effort. Which commitment vehicle is most appropriate?
options: A. Compute Savings Plans purchased in the management account, applied across the organization.
B. Standard Reserved Instances purchased per instance family per Region in each account.
C. EC2 Instance Savings Plans only.
D. Spot Instances for everything regardless of workload tolerance.
explanation: Compute Savings Plans cover EC2, Fargate, and Lambda across families/sizes/Regions and are managed at the org level. Standard RIs are inflexible (B). EC2 Instance SPs lock to a family (C). Spot doesn't fit steady-state workloads (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Compute Savings Plans cover EC2/Fargate/Lambda across any family, size, OS, and Region, and apply across linked accounts when bought in the management account.
Standard RIs are bound to a specific family and Region with little flexibility, contradicting the 'maximum flexibility' goal.
EC2 Instance Savings Plans lock you to one instance family per Region, less flexible than Compute Savings Plans.
Spot Instances are not appropriate for steady-state production workloads that cannot tolerate interruption.

question: A long-running EC2 workload writes 4 TB/day of telemetry to a single gp3 volume. IOPS are saturated; the team has already raised IOPS to 16,000 and throughput to 1000 MB/s without effect. What is the proper next step?
options: A. Migrate the volume to io2 Block Express or stripe multiple gp3 volumes via LVM; move long-term data to S3 with Kinesis Data Firehose and shrink the working set on EBS.
B. Increase the volume size only; IOPS scale linearly with capacity on gp3.
C. Switch to st1 for higher throughput.
D. Move to instance-store ephemeral disks on the same instance class.
explanation: io2 Block Express scales to high IOPS on supported instances; striping gp3 also helps; offloading cold telemetry to S3 reduces hot working set. gp3 IOPS are decoupled from size and cap at 16k for a single volume (B). st1 is throughput-optimized HDD with low IOPS (C). Instance-store loses durability (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — io2 Block Express scales to far higher IOPS on supported instance families; alternatively LVM-striping multiple gp3 volumes is a quick win, and shipping cold telemetry to S3 via Firehose shrinks the hot working set.
gp3 IOPS are not coupled to size and cap at 16k for a single volume; increasing size does not unlock more IOPS.
st1 is throughput-optimized HDD with low random IOPS and is the wrong direction for an IOPS-bound workload.
Instance-store loses data on stop/terminate and does not provide durable storage for telemetry.

question: A logging service uses a single CloudWatch Logs group with hundreds of millions of events per day. Searches via Logs Insights are slow and expensive. Which migration improves analytics performance without losing live tail capability?
options: A. Subscribe the log group to a Kinesis Data Firehose that delivers to S3 in Parquet (with Lambda transformation), partition daily, query via Athena; keep CloudWatch Logs retention short for live tail and deep-history searches go to Athena.
B. Increase Logs Insights query timeout and run parallel queries.
C. Store all logs in CloudWatch Logs forever and use Logs Insights exclusively.
D. Replace CloudWatch Logs entirely with X-Ray.
explanation: Subscriptions to Firehose-to-S3 in Parquet and Athena gives cheap, fast historical analytics; CloudWatch retains short for live tail. Logs Insights doesn't scale to billions of events economically (B, C). X-Ray is for tracing, not log analytics (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — a Firehose subscription delivering Parquet to S3 (with Lambda transform) plus partition projection in Athena gives cheap fast historical queries, while short CloudWatch Logs retention preserves live tail.
Longer timeouts and parallelism do not change the cost model of Logs Insights at hundreds of millions of events/day.
Keeping all logs in CloudWatch Logs forever is one of the most expensive log storage choices and scans grow linearly.
X-Ray is a distributed tracing service, not a log analytics platform; it does not replace structured log search.

question: A microservices stack on ECS Fargate frequently sees task failures because ECR pulls saturate the NAT Gateway during deploys. Which optimization is best?
options: A. Use VPC interface endpoints (PrivateLink) for ECR API and ECR DKR plus a Gateway endpoint for S3, and enable image pull through cache for frequently-used base images.
B. Increase NAT Gateway count to four per AZ.
C. Schedule deploys overnight to avoid contention.
D. Move all images to Docker Hub to bypass ECR.
explanation: ECR endpoints + S3 gateway endpoint route image pulls inside the VPC, removing NAT pressure entirely; pull-through cache reduces external pulls. More NAT Gateways still pay per-byte and don't fix the data-transfer cost (B). Scheduling shifts the problem (C). Docker Hub adds external dependency and rate limits (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — interface endpoints for ECR API and ECR DKR plus an S3 gateway endpoint keep image pulls on the AWS private network, removing NAT pressure entirely; pull-through cache cuts external pulls.
Adding NAT Gateways doesn't change per-byte data-processing cost and still routes image traffic through NAT unnecessarily.
Scheduling deploys overnight just shifts the contention, it doesn't address the root cause.
Docker Hub adds an external dependency with its own rate limits and increases — not decreases — egress to the internet.

question: A team's existing Aurora PostgreSQL cluster periodically hits storage IO bottlenecks during analytics queries that scan multi-GB tables. Online transactional reads are degraded. Which improvement isolates analytics without splitting the database?
options: A. Enable Aurora Optimized Reads (with local NVMe cache on r6id-class instances) for analytics queries, route those queries to dedicated reader endpoints in a custom endpoint group.
B. Migrate to Redshift and run all OLTP through Redshift Spectrum.
C. Add more writer instances; Aurora supports multiple writers automatically.
D. Switch to RDS for PostgreSQL with provisioned IOPS.
explanation: Optimized Reads uses local NVMe to satisfy temporary tables and large scans, isolating analytics IO; custom endpoints route specific consumers. Redshift can't run OLTP (B). Aurora has a single writer per cluster (Aurora MySQL multi-master was a limited-preview feature and Aurora PostgreSQL does not offer it) (C). RDS PIOPS doesn't match Aurora's storage architecture and increases cost (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Aurora Optimized Reads on r6id-class instances uses local NVMe to satisfy temp tables and large scans, isolating analytics IO from OLTP; custom endpoint groups route those queries to the right readers.
Redshift is not an OLTP engine; running OLTP through Spectrum is not a workable architecture.
Aurora PostgreSQL has a single writer per cluster; multi-writer is not a generally available option on PostgreSQL.
RDS Provisioned IOPS uses a different storage architecture and does not give Aurora-equivalent performance; it also increases cost without solving the analytics isolation problem.

question: A production workload depends on an application's correct behavior during partial Region outages of a single AZ. The team wants to validate this proactively each quarter. Which AWS service supports designed chaos experiments with safety guardrails?
options: A. AWS Fault Injection Service with experiment templates that target specific AZ subnets and CloudWatch alarms acting as stop conditions.
B. AWS Trusted Advisor's reliability checks executed on a schedule.
C. CloudWatch Synthetics canaries simulating user traffic only.
D. AWS Resilience Hub recommendations with no experiment execution.
explanation: Fault Injection Service is purpose-built; templates and stop conditions provide safety. Trusted Advisor flags configuration issues, not failure injection (B). Canaries test from outside but don't inject failure (C). Resilience Hub assesses, but FIS is what runs the experiments (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — AWS Fault Injection Service runs designed chaos experiments with templates that target specific resources (e.g., an AZ's subnets) and CloudWatch alarm-based stop conditions for safety.
Trusted Advisor flags configuration issues; it does not inject failures.
CloudWatch Synthetics generates traffic from outside; it cannot inject infrastructure faults.
Resilience Hub assesses target RTO/RPO and policy; FIS is what actually runs the experiments.

question: A platform's CI/CD pipeline pushes 50 deploys/day across many accounts. Lead time and rollback speed are the priority. Which combination yields fastest, safest deploys?
options: A. AWS CodePipeline with multi-account deployment via cross-account roles, blue/green via CodeDeploy on Lambda or ECS, automated canary alarms in CloudWatch tied to rollback triggers, and deployment configuration that aborts on alarm.
B. Manual approvals at every stage for every account.
C. Push directly to production from a developer machine with kubectl apply.
D. Single mega-monolith pipeline with no environment separation.
explanation: Cross-account CodePipeline + CodeDeploy blue/green + alarm-driven rollbacks is the AWS reference pattern. Manual approvals every stage destroy lead time (B). Direct kubectl from laptops is anti-pattern (C). Mono-pipeline is risky (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — cross-account CodePipeline, CodeDeploy blue/green (Lambda/ECS), and CloudWatch alarm-driven rollback give fast deploys with safe automated rollback.
Manual approvals at every stage in every account destroys lead time and adds inconsistent human variance.
kubectl-from-laptop direct to prod has no audit, no review, and no automated rollback — an explicit anti-pattern.
A single mega-monolith pipeline lacks environment separation and risk-isolates poorly.

question: A Lambda-backed API has bursty workload that occasionally exceeds the account's per-Region concurrency limit, causing throttles. The team can't change the function logic. Which combination addresses the symptom and the root cause?
options: A. Request a Service Quotas concurrency increase, set Reserved Concurrency on the critical function to guarantee a slice, use SQS as a buffer with a DLQ for spillover, and enable Lambda asynchronous invocation with destinations.
B. Add CloudFront caching in front and ignore throttles.
C. Increase function memory to 10 GB to make it run faster.
D. Move to Lambda@Edge to spread across more PoPs.
explanation: Quota increase plus Reserved Concurrency for critical workloads plus an SQS buffer is the standard pattern. CloudFront caches don't help dynamic non-cacheable APIs (B). Larger memory might shorten duration but doesn't add concurrency slots (C). Lambda@Edge has stricter concurrency limits and isn't a fit for backend APIs (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — raising the Service Quotas concurrency limit addresses the root cause, Reserved Concurrency guarantees a slice for the critical function, and an SQS buffer plus async destinations smooths bursts without losing events.
CloudFront caching does not help dynamic, per-user API responses and does nothing to relieve concurrency exhaustion.
More memory makes invocations finish faster but does not add concurrency slots — the limit is on concurrent executions, not duration.
Lambda@Edge has stricter concurrency caps and runtime limits and is not appropriate for backend APIs that need this much capacity.

question: An existing data warehouse uses Redshift provisioned dc2 nodes 24x7. Utilization is 12% during business hours and near-zero off-hours. Which optimization keeps SQL compatibility but reduces cost most?
options: A. Migrate to Amazon Redshift Serverless and use it with workgroups and per-user usage limits; data sharing makes the migration low-risk.
B. Pause and resume the cluster nightly via Lambda.
C. Switch to Athena and drop Redshift entirely.
D. Buy 3-year Redshift reserved nodes for the dc2 sizes.
explanation: Redshift Serverless bills only for compute used and matches the SQL surface; data sharing lets you migrate without copying. Pause/resume helps but provisioned still has minimums (B). Athena replacement may not match Redshift's perf/feature surface (C). Reservation locks in over-provisioning (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Redshift Serverless bills only for compute used (RPU-seconds), matches the Redshift SQL surface, and Redshift data sharing makes the migration low-risk; per-workgroup usage limits cap spend.
Pause/resume nightly still keeps the cluster provisioned during business hours at 12% utilization, leaving substantial waste.
Athena's SQL surface and concurrency profile differ from Redshift; not a like-for-like swap for a warehouse.
Reserving over-provisioned dc2 nodes locks in waste and dc2 is an older generation.

question: An existing workload sends 5 TB/day of CloudWatch metrics; the bill from CloudWatch is the largest line item. Most of the metrics are never viewed. Which is the right combination to keep observability while cutting cost most?
options: A. Tier metrics: keep only essential dashboards/alarms in CloudWatch, send high-cardinality custom metrics via the Embedded Metric Format (EMF) so they share log ingestion costs, and forward bulk metrics to a TSDB like Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus or Timestream as appropriate.
B. Disable all CloudWatch metrics across the account.
C. Move all metrics to X-Ray.
D. Buy a discount on CloudWatch from your account team and call it done.
explanation: Tiering plus EMF (which charges via Logs ingestion, often cheaper for high-cardinality dimensions) plus AMP for Prometheus-style time series is the standard cost pattern. Disabling all metrics breaks ops (B). X-Ray is tracing, not metrics (C). Negotiated rates don't fix the architecture (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — tiering metrics (essential alarms/dashboards in CloudWatch, high-cardinality via EMF that bills as Logs, bulk metrics into AMP/Timestream) cuts cost while preserving observability.
Disabling all CloudWatch metrics removes alarms, dashboards, and detection — breaking operations.
X-Ray is a tracing service and does not store metrics; it cannot replace CloudWatch metrics.
A pricing discount may help marginally but does not change the architecture-level cost driver.

question: A company is decomposing a legacy monolith into services and adopting an event-driven architecture: domain events from order, payment, and shipping services need fan-out to many consumers, schema evolution, replay, and selective filtering. Which combo is best?
options: A. Amazon EventBridge with a custom event bus per domain, schema registry for discovery and code-binding generation, archive + replay for bus history, and content-based rule filtering for consumers.
B. SNS topics per event type with SQS subscribers and ad-hoc Schemas in Confluence.
C. Kinesis Data Streams per service with consumer Lambdas filtering on payload via a Glue catalog.
D. A single SQS Standard queue per consumer; producers fan out via cross-account IAM.
explanation: EventBridge offers content-based filtering, archive/replay, and a schema registry — purpose-built for this. SNS+SQS lacks filtering as expressive as EventBridge and there's no managed schema registry there (B). Kinesis is for stream processing, not pub/sub fan-out with filters (C). Direct SQS fan-out scales poorly and lacks schema/replay (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — EventBridge offers content-based rule filtering, an archive+replay feature, and a Schema Registry with code-binding generation, all of which directly match the requirements.
SNS supports basic message filtering but lacks EventBridge's expressive content-based rules; Confluence is not a managed schema registry.
Kinesis Data Streams is a stream-processing primitive, not a pub/sub fan-out service with rule-based filtering and replay.
Per-consumer SQS Standard queues with producer fan-out scales poorly, lacks schema management, and provides no replay or filtering.

question: A retail platform is migrating from on-prem ESB-based B2B integration to AWS and must connect with 40 partner APIs across REST, SOAP, SFTP, and async webhooks. The team wants managed connectors and orchestration to accelerate the move. Which AWS service catalog fits?
options: A. Amazon AppFlow for SaaS connector flows, AWS Transfer Family for SFTP, EventBridge Pipes for filtered cross-service plumbing, and Step Functions for orchestrating end-to-end.
B. Single Lambda per partner; bespoke code for everything; no managed connectors.
C. AWS DataSync for all partner data transfers regardless of protocol.
D. AWS Glue Studio for all integrations including SOAP.
explanation: AppFlow + Transfer Family + EventBridge Pipes + Step Functions is the AWS-native suite for B2B integration. Per-partner Lambdas (B) eschew managed connectors. DataSync is for storage migration (C). Glue Studio is for ETL, not SOAP integrations (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — AppFlow covers many SaaS connectors, Transfer Family handles SFTP, EventBridge Pipes does filtered point-to-point plumbing with transforms, and Step Functions orchestrates end-to-end.
Hand-rolled Lambdas per partner abandon the goal of using managed connectors and slow the migration.
DataSync is for storage-to-storage data movement, not for B2B partner API integration.
Glue Studio is for ETL pipelines; it is not a SOAP integration platform.

question: A team migrates from on-prem to AWS using Application Migration Service. Some servers boot but applications fail to come up. Which combination of post-launch validations should be standard?
options: A. Use MGN post-launch actions to run validation scripts (e.g., boot-time service checks, SSM patch baseline scans, reachability tests), include integration tests via CodeBuild/Step Functions, and validate cutover with replication-resync if needed.
B. Trust that any server that boots is migrated successfully.
C. Skip validation on the assumption that AWS hardware is identical to on-prem.
D. Re-create the server from scratch via AMI rebake.
explanation: MGN post-launch actions exist exactly for this; combine with custom integration tests for confidence. Trust-without-validation is unsafe (B, C). Re-creating defeats lift-and-shift (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — MGN post-launch actions run SSM-driven validation scripts after each test/cutover; combine with CodeBuild/Step Functions integration tests and re-sync if needed.
Booting is necessary but not sufficient for application-level correctness; many app-layer failures still occur post-boot.
AWS hardware and drivers differ from on-prem; this assumption is exactly why post-launch validation exists.
Re-creating from scratch defeats the lift-and-shift premise of MGN and discards captured server state.

question: An enterprise needs to migrate a 4 PB on-prem object store to S3. They have a 1 Gbps internet connection and a 30-day deadline. Which approach is feasible?
options: A. AWS Snowball Edge devices (in parallel) for the bulk move, with ongoing changes shipped via DataSync over a temporary Direct Connect or VPN; perform post-cutover delta validation.
B. Use AWS Storage Gateway file mode and let it sync over 1 Gbps for 30 days.
C. Compress all data and ship via FTP to S3.
D. Use S3 Multi-Region Access Points to import-from-on-prem natively.
explanation: At 1 Gbps you cannot transfer 4 PB online in 30 days; physical Snow devices (Snowball Edge in parallel; Snowmobile has been retired for new customers but the concept of physical shipping applies) plus a delta sync is the standard. Storage Gateway over 1 Gbps will fail the deadline (B). FTP to S3 isn't a thing (C). Multi-Region Access Points don't import from on-prem (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — at 1 Gbps you cannot transfer 4 PB online in 30 days (theoretical max ~324 TB/30 days), so multiple Snowball Edge devices ship the bulk and DataSync replicates the deltas during cutover.
Storage Gateway file mode over a 1 Gbps link cannot move 4 PB in 30 days even at line rate.
FTP is not a supported S3 ingest mechanism; compression alone does not solve a fundamental bandwidth shortfall.
S3 Multi-Region Access Points route requests across Regions; they do not import data from on-prem.

question: A team is migrating a SQL Server 2014 database to Aurora PostgreSQL. They need to convert schema and code, replicate data with low downtime, and validate. Which AWS combination?
options: A. AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for schema and code conversion (with action items for incompatible objects); AWS DMS with full-load + CDC; DMS data validation tasks; cutover after validation.
B. Native SQL Server backup + restore to Aurora.
C. AWS Backup cross-engine restore from SQL Server to Aurora.
D. Manually rewrite the schema and bulk-export to CSV via S3.
explanation: SCT + DMS + DMS validation is the AWS-canonical heterogeneous migration. SQL Server native backups don't restore to a different engine (B). AWS Backup doesn't change engines (C). Manual rewrite skips automation that SCT provides (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — SCT converts schema and T-SQL with action items for incompatible objects, DMS performs full-load + CDC to minimize downtime, and DMS data validation tasks verify row-level fidelity.
SQL Server native backups can only be restored to SQL Server, not to a Postgres engine.
AWS Backup is for AWS-resource backup; it does not transform schemas across engines.
Manually rewriting schemas and re-importing CSVs skips all the conversion and validation tooling SCT and DMS provide.

question: A monolithic Java app on Tomcat must be containerized as part of modernization with minimal code change. Which AWS tool provides automated container packaging and IaC generation?
options: A. AWS App2Container.
B. AWS Elastic Beanstalk.
C. AWS Proton.
D. AWS CodeArtifact.
explanation: App2Container analyzes Java/.NET apps and packages them as containers with Kubernetes/ECS deployment artifacts. Beanstalk is a PaaS but doesn't analyze and package monoliths the same way (B). Proton is platform templates (C). CodeArtifact is package management (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — App2Container inspects existing Java/.NET applications, packages them as Docker images, and produces ECS/EKS deployment artifacts and IaC.
Elastic Beanstalk is a PaaS for deploying applications, but it does not analyze existing monoliths and emit container artifacts.
Proton publishes platform templates for self-service environments; it is not an analyzer/packager.
CodeArtifact is a package repository for dependencies, not an application containerization tool.

question: A bank wants to break a monolith into microservices using the Strangler Fig pattern with no Big Bang cutover. Which AWS service is best to incrementally route specific URL paths to new microservices?
options: A. Amazon API Gateway with route-level integrations: legacy paths route to the monolith, new paths route to microservices behind ALB or Lambda; gradually shift more paths.
B. Application Load Balancer rules can do path-based routing too, with the monolith as a default target group and microservices as additional target groups for new paths.
C. AWS App Mesh sidecars on every microservice with no central routing.
D. CloudFront behaviors at the edge to split paths between monolith and microservices.
explanation: ALB path-based routing is the simplest, lowest-latency Strangler Fig pattern within a VPC; you swap legacy paths to new target groups gradually. API Gateway works too (A) but adds latency and is best when you need API features such as auth/throttling/transformations; for a pure routing strangler, ALB is more efficient. App Mesh handles east-west service mesh, not external routing (C). CloudFront splits at the edge but is overkill if traffic is internal (D).
optionExplanations: API Gateway works but adds per-request overhead and is overkill when the requirement is purely path-based routing between a monolith and new microservices.
Correct — ALB path-based routing rules let you redirect specific URL paths from the monolith target group to new microservice target groups one at a time, with the lowest latency overhead.
App Mesh provides east-west service-mesh features; it does not perform external Strangler-style routing.
CloudFront behaviors can split paths at the edge but are overkill and add edge round-trips when traffic is largely internal.

question: An IT discovery is needed before migration: dependencies, performance, and license posture for 3,000 on-prem servers. Which AWS service is the right starting point?
options: A. AWS Application Discovery Service (Agentless Collector and/or agents) integrated with Migration Hub for portfolio analysis.
B. AWS Trusted Advisor.
C. AWS Compute Optimizer.
D. AWS Systems Manager Inventory only.
explanation: Application Discovery Service is purpose-built for pre-migration discovery and Migration Hub aggregates findings. Trusted Advisor inspects existing AWS, not on-prem (B). Compute Optimizer needs AWS workloads (C). SSM Inventory is for already-managed instances, not pre-migration discovery at scale (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Application Discovery Service (agentless or agent-based) is purpose-built for pre-migration discovery of on-prem dependencies, performance, and license posture, with Migration Hub for portfolio analysis.
Trusted Advisor inspects existing AWS accounts; it does not discover on-prem servers.
Compute Optimizer operates on AWS workloads to recommend sizes; it is not an on-prem discovery tool.
SSM Inventory works on already-managed nodes and isn't designed for pre-migration on-prem fleet discovery at scale.

question: A company is migrating a Windows file server with DFS-R replication to AWS, retaining SMB clients. Which AWS storage is the most appropriate?
options: A. Amazon FSx for Windows File Server with multi-AZ deployment and DFS Namespaces for federated naming.
B. Amazon EFS with Windows clients.
C. Amazon S3 with the SMB protocol enabled.
D. Amazon FSx for Lustre.
explanation: FSx for Windows File Server (multi-AZ) is the SMB-native managed offering and supports DFS Namespaces. EFS is NFS, not SMB (B). S3 has no SMB (C). FSx for Lustre is HPC, not SMB file shares (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — FSx for Windows File Server is SMB-native, supports multi-AZ deployments, and integrates with DFS Namespaces for federated names.
EFS is NFSv4 (no native SMB) and is not the right fit for Windows file-server workloads.
S3 does not natively expose SMB; you would need a gateway, which adds latency and breaks DFS-style semantics.
FSx for Lustre is a high-performance parallel filesystem for HPC scratch, not Windows SMB workloads.

question: A retailer's mainframe runs COBOL batch jobs. They want to migrate to AWS without rewriting in Java day one. Which AWS service set helps?
options: A. AWS Mainframe Modernization with the Micro Focus replatform engine for re-hosting COBOL on managed compute, or the AWS Blu Age refactor engine for automated transformation to Java; AWS DMS for VSAM/IMS data; AWS Migration Hub Refactor Spaces to incrementally extract APIs.
B. App Runner with a custom COBOL runtime image.
C. Lambda with a COBOL custom runtime layer.
D. AWS Elastic Beanstalk with a Node.js platform.
explanation: Mainframe Modernization explicitly offers Micro Focus (replatform) and Blu Age (refactor) engines, with DMS for mainframe data and Refactor Spaces for incremental API extraction. The other answers don't address mainframe artifacts (B, C, D).
optionExplanations: Correct — AWS Mainframe Modernization offers Micro Focus (replatform) for re-hosting COBOL and Blu Age (refactor) for automated transformation; DMS handles VSAM/IMS data and Refactor Spaces extracts APIs incrementally.
App Runner runs containerized web apps; it does not host or migrate mainframe COBOL batch.
Lambda custom runtimes do not address mainframe transaction monitors, JCL, or batch job semantics.
Elastic Beanstalk Node.js is unrelated to COBOL/mainframe migration.

question: An organization wants to migrate VMware on-prem VMs as-is to AWS, retaining VMware management tooling for a transition period. Which AWS option is best?
options: A. A VMware-on-AWS dedicated SDDC offering (e.g., VMware Cloud on AWS or its current successor service) for VMware-native infrastructure on AWS bare metal, then incremental migration to native EC2 via HCX or AWS MGN.
B. Convert every VM to a Lambda function.
C. Lift VMs to Amazon WorkSpaces.
D. Run vSphere on EC2 t3.medium instances.
explanation: A VMware-on-AWS SDDC offering keeps existing tooling and SDDC; HCX or MGN then move to native AWS over time. Lambda doesn't run VMs (B). WorkSpaces is for end-user desktops (C). vSphere on shared-tenant EC2 isn't supported the way bare-metal SDDC is (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — a VMware-on-AWS SDDC (e.g., VMware Cloud on AWS or its current successor) keeps existing vSphere tooling; HCX or AWS MGN then migrate workloads to native EC2 incrementally.
Lambda does not run VMs; conversion of arbitrary on-prem VMs to Lambda functions is not feasible.
WorkSpaces provides managed virtual desktops to end users, not a VM migration target for server workloads.
vSphere is not supported on shared-tenant EC2 t3 instances; the supported offering uses dedicated bare-metal SDDC.

question: A regulated workload requires AWS services to run on customer premises in a specific datacenter, with the same APIs and a low-latency local plane. Which service is correct?
options: A. AWS Outposts (rack or servers) provisioned in the customer datacenter, providing a subset of AWS services with the same APIs.
B. AWS Wavelength.
C. AWS Local Zones.
D. Amazon CloudFront.
explanation: Outposts brings AWS infrastructure on-premises. Wavelength embeds compute at telco 5G edge sites (B). Local Zones are AWS-managed metros (C). CloudFront is a CDN (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — AWS Outposts (rack or 1U/2U servers) installs in the customer datacenter and exposes a subset of AWS services with the same APIs as Regions.
Wavelength embeds compute at telecom 5G edge sites, not in customer datacenters.
Local Zones are AWS-operated metro extensions of a Region, not on customer premises.
CloudFront is a CDN delivered from AWS edge locations; it is not on-premises infrastructure.

question: A company runs Kubernetes in a corporate datacenter and wants AWS to manage the control plane while they keep workers on-prem. Which AWS service is appropriate?
options: A. Amazon EKS Hybrid Nodes (the AWS-managed control plane in-Region with worker nodes joined from on-premises), or EKS Anywhere for a fully on-prem distribution registered to EKS for visibility.
B. Amazon ECS Anywhere only.
C. AWS Fargate on Outposts only.
D. EKS Distro deployed manually with no AWS control plane.
explanation: Where the requirement is specifically 'AWS manages the control plane, workers stay on-prem', EKS Hybrid Nodes (announced in 2024) joins on-prem worker nodes to an AWS-managed EKS control plane. EKS Anywhere runs Kubernetes entirely on customer infrastructure (control plane on-prem). ECS Anywhere is for ECS, not Kubernetes (B). Fargate on Outposts uses Outpost hardware in the customer datacenter (C). EKS Distro is just the open-source distribution with no managed service (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — EKS Hybrid Nodes pairs an AWS-managed EKS control plane with worker nodes running in the customer datacenter, fitting the stated requirement; EKS Anywhere is a related option for fully on-prem operation.
ECS Anywhere is for ECS, not Kubernetes.
Fargate on Outposts requires AWS Outpost hardware, not arbitrary customer datacenters, and is for ECS/EKS workers on the Outpost.
EKS Distro is the open-source upstream distribution with no managed control plane from AWS.

question: An enterprise migration requires moving 200 TB of files weekly from a regional NAS to S3 with bandwidth throttling and resumable transfers. Which AWS service is the best fit?
options: A. AWS DataSync with bandwidth limits, scheduled tasks, and built-in checksum verification.
B. rsync over SSH from a Windows file share.
C. AWS Storage Gateway File Mode (file gateway) only.
D. AWS Transfer Family SFTP server.
explanation: DataSync is purpose-built with throttling, scheduling, integrity checks, and resume. rsync over Windows is brittle (B). File Gateway is for NFS/SMB-fronted access not bulk migration (C). Transfer Family is SFTP for inbound transfer use cases, not large NAS sync (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — DataSync supports bandwidth throttling, scheduling, resume, and built-in checksum verification — designed exactly for periodic NAS-to-S3 migrations.
rsync over SSH from Windows is brittle, manually managed, and lacks AWS-side integrity verification.
Storage Gateway file mode is for ongoing NFS/SMB-fronted access from on-prem, not weekly bulk migrations of hundreds of TB.
Transfer Family provides managed SFTP/FTP/FTPS endpoints; it is not a NAS-to-S3 migration tool.

question: A company wants to publish a SaaS product to multiple customer AWS accounts. They want to expose a single API endpoint that customers can connect to privately, without traversing the internet, while keeping customers isolated from each other. Which AWS pattern is correct?
options: A. AWS PrivateLink: publish a Network Load Balancer-fronted VPC Endpoint Service in the SaaS account; customers create Interface VPC Endpoints to reach the service privately, with each connection isolated by endpoint.
B. Share the SaaS VPC across customer accounts via AWS RAM and let them peer freely.
C. Public ALB with IP allow-listing per customer.
D. CloudFront with custom origins per customer.
explanation: PrivateLink (Endpoint Services) is the AWS-blessed SaaS connectivity pattern: customers privately connect via interface endpoints with no cross-customer reachability. Sharing a VPC is anti-pattern for tenant isolation (B). Public + allow-list traverses the internet (C). CloudFront also uses the internet (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — PrivateLink with an NLB-backed VPC Endpoint Service gives each customer a private interface endpoint to the SaaS, with no cross-customer reachability and no public internet exposure.
Sharing the SaaS VPC across customer accounts breaks tenant isolation and is an anti-pattern.
A public ALB with IP allow-listing still traverses the internet, missing the 'without internet' requirement.
CloudFront also routes traffic over the public internet from the viewer to the edge; it doesn't satisfy private connectivity.

question: A logistics customer needs ultra-low-latency compute at telecom 5G edge for AR-based warehouse navigation. Which AWS service should be the foundation?
options: A. AWS Wavelength Zones inside a telecom partner's 5G network for single-digit-millisecond latency to mobile devices.
B. AWS Local Zones in the metro where the warehouse is located.
C. AWS Outposts inside the warehouse.
D. CloudFront with Lambda@Edge.
explanation: Wavelength puts compute at the carrier 5G aggregation site, giving the lowest mobile-to-cloud latency. Local Zones are metros but not in the carrier RAN (B). Outposts is on-prem hardware that you operate (C). Lambda@Edge serves HTTP at CloudFront edges, not 5G mobile compute (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Wavelength Zones embed AWS compute and storage inside the telecom carrier's 5G network, giving single-digit-millisecond round-trip latency to mobile devices on that network.
Local Zones extend a Region into a metro but do not sit inside the carrier's RAN, so latency to a 5G mobile device is higher than Wavelength.
Outposts is customer-operated on-prem hardware; it does not natively provide low-latency access from cellular devices on a carrier network.
CloudFront/Lambda@Edge serve HTTP from CloudFront edges; they are not optimized for 5G mobile compute or AR workloads.

question: A B2B payments company runs its core ledger on a single Aurora PostgreSQL writer in us-east-1 that has scaled vertically to a db.r7g.16xlarge instance. Write throughput has grown 35% year-over-year for six consecutive quarters and a capacity model commissioned by the CFO shows the workload will exceed the largest available instance class within 18 months, before the team's planned hardware refresh window. The CIO has set five non-negotiable constraints. First, keep PostgreSQL wire-protocol compatibility so the existing 240-service Spring Boot fleet does not need a rewrite. Second, preserve ACID semantics for individual transactions within a single customer's account because PCI-DSS auditors review transactional consistency annually. Third, stay within a quarterly infrastructure cost cap, set jointly by Finance and the CIO, that explicitly disallows running multiple full-fat clusters in parallel as a long-term solution. Fourth, RPO must remain under 1 second and RTO under 90 seconds across all 38 production accounts in the Organization. Fifth, the four-engineer platform team that owns the database cannot operate sharding glue indefinitely; the SRE director has refused any solution that adds a permanent self-managed shard fabric. The data team has profiled traffic and found that 90% of transactions are scoped to a single tenant_id, while a small number of tables hold cross-tenant reference data that must stay globally consistent. Which Aurora capability best addresses the projected write ceiling while honoring the constraints?
options: A. Migrate to Aurora Limitless Database, designating tenant_id as the shard key on the high-volume tables and keeping the reference tables as reference (replicated) tables on the shared transaction router fleet.
B. Re-platform onto self-managed Citus on EC2 so the team controls shard placement and rebalancing directly, fronted by PgBouncer for connection pooling.
C. Adopt Aurora PostgreSQL with read replicas in the same Region and route writes by tenant to a hand-coded application-level shard map maintained by the platform team.
D. Migrate the ledger to DynamoDB with tenant_id as the partition key, accepting eventual consistency and rewriting transactional logic with TransactWriteItems.
explanation: Aurora Limitless Database is built exactly for this case: a sharded write tier that still speaks PostgreSQL, with shard keys for tenant-scoped tables and reference tables for globally consistent data, all managed by AWS so a four-engineer team is not on the hook for shard rebalancing. Self-managed Citus shifts undifferentiated operational load onto the small team (B). Application-level sharding is a long-lived glue layer the constraints rule out and read replicas do not scale writes (C). DynamoDB breaks the PostgreSQL wire-protocol constraint and forces a transactional rewrite the CIO disallowed (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Aurora Limitless Database provides horizontal write scale-out under the PostgreSQL wire protocol, with declared shard keys for tenant-scoped tables and reference tables for the small globally consistent dataset, all managed by Aurora so a four-engineer team does not operate the shard fabric.
Self-managed Citus on EC2 puts shard placement, rebalancing, failover, and version upgrades onto the four-engineer platform team — exactly the long-term operational glue the constraints prohibit.

Read replicas do not scale writes, and an application-maintained shard map is the indefinitely-owned glue layer the CIO ruled out; it also breaks ACID for cross-shard transactions without a coordinator.
DynamoDB violates the PostgreSQL wire-protocol constraint, forces a rewrite of the Spring Boot transactional code, and changes the consistency model the ledger depends on.

question: A multinational manufacturer operates an AWS Cloud WAN core network spanning four Regions — us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1, and sa-east-1 — with roughly 180 VPC attachments distributed across 64 AWS accounts in one Organization and organized into three Cloud WAN segments: prod, non-prod, and shared-services. A new ISO 27001 compliance mandate from the corporate security committee, effective within 90 days, requires that (1) all north-south internet egress from prod VPCs traverse a centralized inspection VPC running a third-party NGFW behind a Gateway Load Balancer with full TLS visibility, and (2) all east-west traffic between the prod and shared-services segments be inspected by the same appliance fleet, while non-prod traffic continues to flow without inspection because the CFO has capped inspection-appliance spend at $480K annualized. The five-engineer network team must implement this without adding per-VPC route table edits to 180 attachments — change-management bandwidth simply does not exist — and the design must survive a Regional failure of the appliance fleet by failing over to the appliance fleet in another Region with an RTO of under 5 minutes. The team has already invested in Cloud WAN as the strategic core; ripping it out is not on the table. Which Cloud WAN construct delivers the required service insertion?
options: A. Define a Network Function Group containing the inspection VPC attachments, use send-via in the Cloud WAN policy to steer prod-to-shared-services and prod-to-internet flows through the NFG, and leave non-prod segment routes untouched; configure cross-Region failover within the NFG.
B. Attach a Transit Gateway to each Region and use TGW route table appliance-mode plus per-attachment static routes to force traffic into the inspection VPC, retiring the Cloud WAN core.
C. Move the inspection appliances into every prod VPC as in-line GWLB endpoints and rely on Cloud WAN segment sharing to mesh prod with shared-services.
D. Use Cloud WAN segment isolation to block prod-to-shared-services traffic entirely, and require application teams to expose services via PrivateLink, which AWS inspects natively.
explanation: Network Function Groups are the Cloud WAN primitive purpose-built for service insertion: you attach the inspection VPCs to an NFG, then use send-via in the core network policy to steer specific segment-to-segment and segment-to-internet flows through it without touching individual attachment route tables. Replacing Cloud WAN with TGW abandons the existing investment and reintroduces per-attachment route plumbing (B). Sprinkling GWLBe in every prod VPC blows up cost and operational surface and doesn't centralize inspection (C). Segment isolation blocks the traffic instead of inspecting it, and PrivateLink is not 'natively inspected' by AWS (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Network Function Groups plus send-via in the Cloud WAN policy steer prod east-west and north-south flows through the centralized appliance fleet without per-attachment route edits, while non-prod stays untouched; NFG attachments across Regions provide the cross-Region failover path.
Replacing Cloud WAN with per-Region TGWs throws away the existing core and reintroduces exactly the per-attachment route-table edits the design must avoid at 180 attachments.
Per-VPC GWLB endpoints in every prod VPC fragment the appliance fleet, multiply cost, and contradict the centralized-inspection requirement.
Segment isolation prevents traffic instead of inspecting it, and PrivateLink does not provide built-in deep-packet firewall inspection.

question: A regulated trading platform runs Aurora PostgreSQL Global Database with the writer in us-east-1 and secondary clusters in eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1, serving 3,200 traders across the three Regions under MiFID II and SEC Rule 17a-4 retention rules. Order-entry traffic is overwhelmingly regional: traders in each Region read and write their own Region's order book about 92% of the time, but a small slice of cross-Region settlement writes is operationally acceptable. Latency-sensitive read-after-write semantics within a Region must be honored — when a trader places an order, the very next query from that trader's session must reflect that order, because compliance review trails would otherwise show a stale ack. The architecture team wants traders in all three Regions to feel as if they have a local writer, but a Region failure cannot cause split-brain or lost committed orders; the CTO has set an RPO of 5 seconds and an RTO of 60 seconds, audited quarterly. The application is allowed to tolerate sub-second lag when a trader occasionally queries another Region's data. The DBA team is six engineers and explicitly refuses any solution that requires them to operate cross-Region logical replication or self-managed conflict resolution. The team currently pays for one global cluster and Finance will not approve a second one. Which Aurora Global Database pattern best fits?
options: A. Enable write forwarding on the eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 secondary clusters; have each Region's app connect to its local secondary endpoint, set aurora_replica_read_consistency to SESSION so a trader reads their own just-forwarded writes, and rely on managed planned/unplanned failover for the primary.
B. Treat all three Regions as independent writers using Aurora multi-master across Regions and reconcile conflicts in a nightly batch job.
C. Run a separate Aurora cluster in every Region and use logical replication with pglogical between them, with the application choosing the closest cluster.
D. Direct every write globally to the us-east-1 writer over the public endpoint and use latency-based Route 53 records to send reads to whichever secondary is closest.
explanation: Aurora Global write forwarding lets secondary clusters accept writes that are proxied to the writer, with session-level consistency modes that let a trader read their own writes after acknowledgement. Cross-Region writes still pay the round-trip, but for the dominant in-Region workload the local secondary serves reads fast and only the settlement slice pays the long-haul write cost. Aurora multi-master across Regions does not exist as a supported topology (B). pglogical between Regions reintroduces conflict handling the team explicitly avoided and is operationally heavy (C). A single writer endpoint with latency-routed reads gives no local-feel writes in EU or APAC and risks stale reads breaking read-your-writes (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — write forwarding gives each Region's application a local endpoint that accepts writes, proxies them to the primary, and offers session consistency modes that preserve read-your-writes for the trader who just submitted an order; managed failover handles the unplanned-Region case without split-brain.
Aurora does not support cross-Region multi-master writers, and reconciling order writes in a nightly batch is unacceptable for a trading platform with seconds-RPO requirements.
Cross-Region pglogical is self-managed, requires explicit conflict resolution, and reintroduces operational overhead the managed Aurora Global service already solves.
A single us-east-1 writer with latency-routed reads makes every EU and APAC trader pay full cross-Region RTT for every order entry and risks stale reads breaking the read-your-writes guarantee.

question: A European bank operating under a national digital-sovereignty mandate codified in domestic law must prove to its national financial regulator that AWS, acting alone, cannot decrypt customer PII at rest under any circumstance — including under legal compulsion in another jurisdiction such as a foreign court order or subpoena. The bank already runs its workloads in the eu-central-1 (Frankfurt) Region across 22 AWS accounts in a single Organization and has a dedicated four-person key-management team that operates a Thales Luna HSM cluster in two Tier-IV datacenters inside the country, replicated synchronously over private fiber. The CIO has set three operational constraints. First, RTO for cryptographic operations is 5 seconds: any longer takes customer-facing payments and core banking journals offline, breaching the regulator's availability SLA. Second, the design must continue using KMS-integrated server-side encryption for S3, RDS, EBS, and SQS so that 180 already-deployed applications do not need to change their crypto path. Third, the annual external audit demands a written attestation that AWS cannot perform decrypts without the bank's HSM, signed by the CISO. The CISO additionally requires that key material never be exportable from the HSMs in plaintext form, that key rotation be initiated by the bank, and that an auditable break-glass procedure exists for the case where the HSM cluster becomes unreachable. Which KMS pattern satisfies the sovereignty mandate while preserving the operational profile?
options: A. Deploy a KMS External Key Store (XKS) in the Frankfurt Region backed by an XKS proxy fronting the on-prem Thales Luna cluster; use XKS keys for the PII-encrypting CMKs in S3, RDS, and EBS, and pre-stage an explicit, audited procedure to fail back to AWS-managed CMKs only with a documented break-glass approval.
B. Use AWS CloudHSM clusters in Frankfurt and import key material there; back KMS CMKs with the imported material via the BYOK flow.
C. Store all data in S3 with SSE-C and have the application supply per-object keys from the on-prem HSM; skip KMS entirely.
D. Use KMS multi-Region keys replicated across Frankfurt and Paris and rotate them every 90 days for sovereignty assurance.
explanation: XKS is the only KMS topology where the key material lives outside AWS and AWS cannot perform a cryptographic operation without a successful round trip to the customer's external HSM via the XKS proxy. CloudHSM in-Region is still AWS-operated hardware and BYOK imports key material into KMS, so AWS can decrypt without external participation (B). SSE-C abandons KMS-integrated services like RDS and EBS encryption that the bank uses (C). Multi-Region keys keep all material inside AWS and rotation does not address sovereignty (D). The break-glass for XKS proxy outage must be explicit and audited because XKS unavailability stops all crypto operations.
optionExplanations: Correct — XKS keeps the master key material in the customer-operated Luna cluster and forces every KMS decrypt to traverse the XKS proxy, so AWS cannot decrypt PII without external HSM participation; the audited break-glass acknowledges that an XKS-proxy outage otherwise halts crypto operations.
CloudHSM is AWS-operated hardware in an AWS Region, and BYOK imports key material into KMS — both leave AWS technically able to decrypt without the external HSM, failing the sovereignty test.
SSE-C requires the client to manage keys per object and abandons KMS-integrated encryption for RDS, EBS, and other services the bank already uses, which is operationally infeasible at this scale.
Multi-Region keys still live entirely inside KMS; replication and rotation do not address whether AWS alone can decrypt the data.

question: A global insurer running 140 AWS accounts under one Organization must satisfy a new FINRA-equivalent retention rule issued by its national insurance regulator: every backup of a regulated workload — including RDS snapshots, EBS volume backups, EFS file system backups, DynamoDB tables, and Aurora cluster snapshots — must be immutable for a minimum of 7 years with no path, even for the organization's root user, a delegated administrator, or AWS Support, to shorten the retention window or delete a recovery point during that interval. The compliance lead has set three explicit requirements. First, the immutability must be enforced consistently across all 140 accounts without per-account configuration drift; auditors have already flagged drift findings from the team's previous attempt using ad-hoc account-level scripts. Second, the platform team — six engineers across two Regions — must be able to prove to auditors via a single API call or screenshot that the configuration is locked, with a clear cooling-off period before the lock takes effect to allow last-minute corrections. Third, the cost overhead of immutability must stay under 8% of the current backup spend so that Finance does not need to renegotiate the annual budget. The backup team currently uses AWS Backup with vaults in each account, but the retention rules on those vaults can be edited by any account-local admin, which is exactly what the regulator now prohibits. Which change closes the gap?
options: A. Apply AWS Backup Vault Lock in compliance mode on the organization's central backup vaults via a delegated administrator and AWS Organizations backup policies, with a cooling-off period and a 7-year minimum retention encoded in the lock.
B. Switch every vault to AWS Backup Vault Lock in governance mode and rely on SCPs to deny vault-lock changes.
C. Replicate every recovery point to an S3 bucket with Object Lock in compliance mode and disable the AWS Backup vaults.
D. Move all backups to AWS Storage Gateway tape virtualization with WORM retention configured on the gateway.
explanation: Vault Lock in compliance mode is the only AWS Backup setting that makes the retention configuration immutable to everyone — including the org root and AWS — for the locked period; applied through Organizations backup policies via a delegated administrator, it enforces consistent settings across all 140 accounts with no drift. Governance mode lets sufficiently-privileged principals modify or delete (B). S3 Object Lock works for raw S3 backups but bypassing AWS Backup loses Backup's cross-service indexing, point-in-time semantics, and the platform team's existing workflow (C). Tape Gateway WORM is for hybrid tape replacement, not org-wide cloud-native backup compliance (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — compliance-mode Vault Lock applied through Organizations backup policies via a delegated administrator yields true WORM retention across the org that even the root user and AWS Support cannot shorten, and the cooling-off period gives auditors a provable lock-in moment.
Governance mode is explicitly mutable by sufficiently-privileged principals; SCPs help but cannot match the cryptographic immutability of compliance-mode Vault Lock that auditors expect.
S3 Object Lock can immutabilize raw objects but abandons AWS Backup's cross-service indexing, point-in-time restore, and cataloging — operationally regressive for 140 accounts.
Storage Gateway tape virtualization is a hybrid migration tool for legacy tape workflows, not an org-wide cloud-native backup retention solution.

question: A media conglomerate with 11 business units — each previously running its own AWS Organization with 18 to 90 member accounts — is consolidating all of them under a single new top-level Organization governed by AWS Control Tower in us-east-1, with workloads spread across us-east-1, us-west-2, and eu-west-1. The platform team is seven engineers and inherits responsibility for vending all new accounts. Leadership has set five constraints. First, every new account must be vended from a GitOps pipeline so that account requests live as pull requests reviewed by both the requesting BU's lead and a central platform reviewer, providing an immutable audit trail to satisfy SOX controls. Second, per-BU baseline customizations — custom VPC CIDR ranges from the BU's IP allocation, BU-specific Service Catalog products, BU-specific SCPs scoped to the BU's OU, and BU-specific Backup vaults — must be expressible as code and versioned per BU. Third, the vending system must roll forward automatically when AWS Control Tower releases new guardrails without forcing the team to re-implement each BU's customizations every quarter. Fourth, the team has deep Terraform expertise but no significant CloudFormation skill and explicitly will not adopt CFN as its primary IaC. Fifth, the CIO has capped this consolidation program at 9 months end-to-end and refuses any approach requiring more than two engineers to operate steady-state. Which approach best fits?
options: A. Use AWS Control Tower Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) with a Git-backed account request pipeline; per-BU customization layers expressed as AFT account-customizations and global-customizations Terraform modules; standard AFT updates pick up new Control Tower guardrails.
B. Hand-roll a Jenkins pipeline that calls the Control Tower API to vend accounts, with per-BU bash scripts that bolt on customizations after vending.
C. Use only Control Tower's built-in Account Factory portal and ask each BU to file Service Catalog requests; track approvals in Jira instead of Git.
D. Skip Control Tower entirely and vend accounts with Organizations CreateAccount API calls from a single Terraform monorepo, applying SCPs and baseline CloudFormation StackSets directly.
explanation: AFT is purpose-built for this: a Git-driven account request pipeline, Terraform modules for global and per-account customizations, and a clean upgrade path as Control Tower evolves. A Jenkins-plus-bash stack reinvents AFT poorly and skips the GitOps review model (B). The portal plus Jira misses GitOps entirely — there is no PR review surface for account intent (C). Skipping Control Tower loses the guardrail framework and forces the team to rebuild landing-zone capability from scratch (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — AFT gives the team a Git-backed account request workflow with PR review, Terraform modules for global and per-BU customization, and a clean upgrade path as Control Tower releases new guardrails — matching every constraint with no hand-rolled glue.
A Jenkins pipeline plus per-BU bash recreates AFT badly, loses the structured Terraform module layers, and is brittle when Control Tower introduces new guardrails.
The Account Factory portal plus Jira-based approvals abandons GitOps and provides no PR-reviewable artifact for account intent or customization.
Skipping Control Tower forfeits the entire landing-zone control plane the conglomerate needs and forces the team to rebuild guardrail enforcement from scratch.

question: A discrete-manufacturing customer running EKS Anywhere on bare-metal Lenovo servers in three factories — in Stuttgart, Monterrey, and Suzhou — wants to consolidate its container platform after a post-mortem showed that 41% of the SRE team's on-call hours over the last six months were spent on Kubernetes-on-bare-metal upkeep (etcd backups, certificate rotation, version upgrades, kube-apiserver outages). The factories run latency-sensitive PLC adapter pods that read/write programmable-logic-controller state with a hard 8-millisecond round-trip budget; these adapter pods must stay on the shop floor and cannot tolerate Regional hairpins. Everything else — control plane, etcd, observability, audit log shipping, and admission webhooks — should move to AWS to free the two-engineer SRE team from operating Kubernetes upgrades, etcd backups, and certificate rotation. Each factory has a redundant 10 Gbps AWS Direct Connect to the nearest Region (eu-central-1, us-east-2, ap-northeast-1) with sub-5-millisecond RTT. The SREs require one EKS console pane to manage all three clusters with consistent IAM, CNI configuration, add-on policy, and OIDC trust. The CIO has set two explicit constraints: total platform cost must not exceed the current EKS Anywhere line item by more than 15%, and the migration must complete within 6 months without any unscheduled shop-floor downtime. Which platform best fits?
options: A. Migrate to Amazon EKS Hybrid Nodes: keep the on-prem servers as worker nodes joined to an AWS-managed EKS control plane over Direct Connect, run shop-floor pods on those nodes via node selectors, and let AWS own the control plane, etcd, and upgrade lifecycle.
B. Stay on EKS Anywhere but contract AWS Enterprise Support to take over upgrades.
C. Move every workload — including the PLC adapters — into EKS in-Region and front the PLCs with WebSocket relays over Direct Connect.
D. Replace Kubernetes with ECS Anywhere on the factory floor; manage from one ECS console in AWS.
explanation: EKS Hybrid Nodes is purpose-built for this profile: the control plane, etcd, and upgrades are AWS-operated, but worker nodes can run on the customer's own hardware on the shop floor and join the cluster over Direct Connect — exactly the split the manufacturer wants. EKS Anywhere keeps the control plane on-prem and AWS Support doesn't operate it for you (B). Moving PLC adapters off the floor adds shop-floor latency that the question explicitly forbids (C). ECS Anywhere is the wrong runtime if the standard is Kubernetes and gives up the Kubernetes ecosystem (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — EKS Hybrid Nodes lets the two-engineer SRE team drop the control-plane, etcd, and upgrade burden onto AWS while keeping latency-sensitive PLC pods on shop-floor hardware, with a single EKS console and consistent IAM/CNI/add-on policy across factories.
EKS Anywhere keeps the control plane on customer hardware regardless of support tier; AWS does not operationally run the control plane for an EKS Anywhere cluster.
Relocating PLC adapters off the shop floor violates the explicit latency constraint, even with Direct Connect, and adds a brittle protocol-relay layer.
ECS Anywhere abandons the Kubernetes standard the team has standardized on and forfeits the Kubernetes ecosystem of operators and tooling already in use.

question: A media company with 4,200 employees across nine offices operates 22 internal web applications — Jira, Confluence, internal dashboards, the build pipeline web UI, observability consoles, an internal HR portal, and a finance application subject to SOX controls — currently fronted by a Cisco AnyConnect VPN concentrator pair and a fleet of 14 EC2 bastion hosts spread across two AWS Regions. After a third-party penetration test flagged the bastion fleet as the company's largest persistent attack surface, the CISO has mandated zero-trust access with three explicit requirements. First, identity must come from the corporate IdP (Okta) via SAML, federated through IAM Identity Center where applicable. Second, device posture — managed-laptop attestation, full-disk encryption status, EDR agent health from CrowdStrike, and OS patch level — must be evaluated on every request, not just at the start of a session. Third, any move to public-internet exposure with WAF IP allow-lists is rejected outright; auditors have already classified that pattern as non-compliant with the company's new zero-trust framework. The platform team additionally wants to retire the bastion fleet entirely to remove SSH key-management toil and eliminate the AnyConnect client install on 4,000 endpoints, which has driven 22% of helpdesk tickets in the last quarter. RTO for access during an IdP outage is 4 hours and is acceptable. The platform team is five engineers. Which AWS service replaces both the VPN and the bastions?
options: A. AWS Verified Access with IAM Identity Center as the trust provider and an EDR/posture trust provider for device signals; per-application Verified Access groups and policies that gate each internal app behind identity-plus-device checks; ALB targets for the apps.
B. AWS Client VPN with SAML federation, keeping the bastion fleet but locking it to MFA-only SSH.
C. Public ALBs for each internal app behind AWS WAF rules that allow only the corporate office IP ranges.
D. AWS App Mesh with mTLS between services, and require employees to install an SPIFFE client on their laptops.
explanation: Verified Access is AWS's purpose-built zero-trust private application gateway: identity from IAM Identity Center or an external SAML IdP, device posture from a partner trust provider (Jamf, CrowdStrike, Jumpcloud, etc.) evaluated on every request, per-application policies, and no VPN client or bastion needed. Client VPN plus bastions retains both the VPN and bastions the team is trying to retire (B). Public ALB plus WAF IP allow-listing exposes the apps to the internet and the CISO rejected that (C). App Mesh is for service-to-service mTLS, not user-to-app access (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Verified Access fronts each internal app with policies that combine IAM Identity Center identity and device-posture trust providers, retiring both the VPN client and the bastion fleet while honoring the no-public-exposure constraint.
Client VPN plus bastions retains exactly the two components the platform team committed to removing and does not evaluate device posture on every request.
Public ALBs with WAF IP allow-lists put the apps on the public internet — the explicit prohibition in the requirements.
App Mesh is a service-to-service mTLS construct for in-cluster traffic, not a user-facing zero-trust application gateway.

question: A financial services group operates two AWS Organizations that must remain legally separate for regulatory reasons — one for the retail bank, subject to consumer-banking supervision, and one for the markets arm, subject to capital-markets supervision — but both are governed by a single group-level security committee that has authority over both. The retail org contains 88 accounts and the markets org contains 54 accounts, with combined workloads across us-east-1, eu-west-1, and ap-southeast-1. Following a near-miss incident in which a developer in the markets org granted cross-account S3 access to an unaffiliated third-party AWS account via a bucket policy, the security committee has issued two overlapping mandates that must take effect within 60 days. First, no IAM principal in either organization may ever assume a role outside the group's curated allow-list of 14 trusted partner account IDs, regardless of which identity policy in any member account permits it. Second, no S3 bucket, KMS key, SQS queue, or Secrets Manager secret in either organization may grant access to a principal whose aws:PrincipalOrgID is not one of the two group org IDs, regardless of what any resource policy says. Both controls must be enforced at the API-call surface — not as after-the-fact findings — and must survive a developer with full administrative IAM permissions inside their own account. The committee has set zero tolerance for drift between the two organizations. Which combination of AWS Organizations policy types implements both mandates?
options: A. Service Control Policies attached at the root of each Organization to deny sts:AssumeRole outside the curated partner-account list, plus Resource Control Policies attached at each root to deny S3, KMS, and SQS access to any principal whose aws:PrincipalOrgID is not in the group's two org IDs.
B. SCPs alone in both Organizations covering both identity and resource constraints by including a Deny on the principal in resource policies via SCP.
C. Permission boundaries on every role in every account, generated by a nightly Lambda that reads the curated partner list from S3.
D. A central IAM Access Analyzer in a delegated admin account that emits findings for any policy violating either mandate, with a Step Functions remediation that revokes the offending policy.
explanation: SCPs are the only policy that bounds what an identity inside the org can do at the API call — they cap sts:AssumeRole regardless of identity policies. RCPs (Resource Control Policies) are the corresponding org-wide cap on what a resource policy in the org can grant — they enforce the resource-perimeter side that SCPs cannot reach. Layering SCPs (identity perimeter) plus RCPs (resource perimeter) is exactly the architecture AWS published for this case. SCPs cannot constrain resource policy grants by themselves (B). Permission boundaries are per-role and brittle across two orgs at this scale (C). Access Analyzer is reactive and the mandates require API-time enforcement (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — SCPs cap what identities inside each Organization can do (the identity perimeter) and RCPs cap what any resource policy in those Organizations can grant (the resource perimeter), and together they enforce both mandates at the API call regardless of identity or resource policies inside a single account.
SCPs do not constrain resource policy grants — a developer can still write a bucket policy granting a non-org principal access, which is exactly what RCPs were introduced to prevent.

Permission boundaries are per-role identity caps that must be re-attached to every new principal in two organizations spanning many accounts; they are operationally brittle and miss the resource-policy gap entirely.
IAM Access Analyzer findings are reactive — the policy violation already exists by the time the finding is emitted, failing the 'enforced at the API call surface' requirement.

question: A national retailer with 90 AWS accounts under one Organization runs about 600 microservices spread across 14 EKS clusters and roughly 2,800 Lambda functions in three Regions, supporting both the customer-facing e-commerce site and the back-office order pipeline. After two costly incidents in which on-call engineers spent 40+ minutes hopping between account-local CloudWatch consoles to correlate a checkout-error spike to a downstream auth service in a different account, SRE leadership has approved a budget to fix the observability gap, but with three explicit requirements. First, each product team must continue to author their own service-level objectives (SLOs) on their own service in their own account, because team autonomy and audit ownership both demand it. Second, the central 12-engineer SRE team must be able to view a single roll-up of SLO health, error-budget burn, and top-talker dependency graphs across the entire estate from one console session — without copying telemetry between accounts or building a parallel ingestion pipeline. Third, on-call dashboards must show request volume, latency percentiles, and error rate for any service in any of the 90 accounts during an incident, drilled in from a single pane. Custom CloudWatch agents are already deployed on every EC2 host and OpenTelemetry traces already feed AWS X-Ray. The CIO has capped this project at three engineer-months and explicitly prohibits any solution that requires the team to build and operate a new ingestion pipeline. Which design fits?
options: A. Enable CloudWatch Application Signals in every account to auto-discover services and emit standardized request/latency/error metrics; configure CloudWatch cross-account observability with the central SRE account as the monitoring account and all 90 accounts as source accounts; author SLOs in each source account and view the org-wide roll-up plus per-service drilldown from the monitoring account.

B. Pipe every account's CloudWatch metrics through Kinesis Data Streams to a central Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus workspace and build Grafana dashboards manually for each of the 600 services.
C. Replicate all CloudWatch metric data to a single account via CloudWatch metric streams and rebuild Application Signals' service maps and SLO logic in an in-house Lambda.
D. Have each product team export metrics to Amazon Timestream in their own account and federate queries via Athena across all 90 Timestream databases.
explanation: Application Signals is the AWS-managed SLO/service-map feature on top of CloudWatch and OTel/X-Ray; combined with CloudWatch cross-account observability (monitoring account + source accounts) it gives a single-pane view across the whole Organization without copying telemetry. Streaming everything to a central AMP workspace plus hand-built Grafana dashboards re-implements Application Signals' SLO and topology features (B). Re-implementing service maps and SLO logic in-house is exactly the bespoke ingestion code the team wants to avoid (C). Timestream is not the right store for distributed-trace SLO computation across 90 accounts and Athena federation is the wrong tool for live SRE dashboards (D).
optionExplanations: Correct — Application Signals auto-discovers services and produces standardized SLO-ready metrics; cross-account observability with the SRE account as the monitoring account and all 90 accounts as source accounts gives org-wide roll-ups and per-service drilldown with no bespoke ingestion code.
Streaming metrics to a central Prometheus workspace and hand-building Grafana dashboards for 600 services duplicates what Application Signals provides natively and adds permanent maintenance burden.
Re-implementing service maps and SLO computation in Lambda is exactly the bespoke ingestion code the team wants to avoid and will diverge from the AWS-native experience over time.
Timestream is a time-series metrics database, not a distributed-trace SLO engine, and cross-account Athena federation is the wrong access pattern for live on-call dashboards.
